texto: God is not only the God of the sufferers but the God who suffers. ... It is said of God that no one can behold his face and live. I always thought this meant that no one could see his splendor and live. A friend said perhaps it meant that no one could see his sorrow and live. Or perhaps his sorrow is splendor. ... Instead of explaining our suffering God shares it.

I always thought this meant that no one could see his splendor and live. A friend said perhaps it meant that no one could see his sorrow and live. Or perhaps his sorrow is splendor. ... Instead of explaining our suffering God shares it.

texto: But we all suffer. For we all prize and love; and in this present existence of ours, prizing and loving yield suffering. Love in our world is suffering love. Some do not suffer much, though, for they do not love much. Suffering is for the loving. This, said Jesus, is the command of the Holy One: "You shall love your neighbor as yourself." In commanding us to love, God invites us to suffer.

texto: And what of regrets? I shall live with them. I shall accept my regrets as part of my life, to be numbered among my self-inflicted wounds. But I will not endlessly gaze at them. I shall allow the memories to prod me into doing better with those still living. And I shall allow them to sharpen the vision and intensify the hope for that Great Day coming when we can all throw ourselves into each other's arms and say, "I'm sorry.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Nicholas Wolterstorff que provoca sentido da existência e experiência vivida. Ao questionar “And what of regrets? I shall live with them. I shall accept my regrets as part of my life, to be …”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre vida, forgiveness, inspiracional, com foco em inspiracao e termino.

texto: Faith is a footbridge that you don't know will hold you up over the chasm until you're forced to walk out onto it.

texto: The tears of God are the meaning of history.

texto: Technology does make possible advance toward shalom; progress in mastery of the world can bring shalom nearer. But the limits of technology must also be acknowledged; technology is entirely incapable of bringing about shalom between ourselves and God, and it is only scarcely capable of bringing about the love of self and neighbour.
explicacao: Orientação direta de Nicholas Wolterstorff sobre vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. texto: How is faith to endure, O God, when you allow all this scraping and tearing on us? You have allowed rivers of blood to flow, mountains of suffering to pile up, sobs to become humanity's song--all without lifting a finger that we could see. You have allowed bonds of love beyond number to be painfully snapped. If you have not abandoned us, explain yourself.We strain to hear. But instead of hearing an answer we catch sight of God himself scraped and torn. Through our tears we see the tears of God.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Nicholas Wolterstorff que provoca vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. Ao questionar “How is faith to endure, O God, when you allow all this scraping and tearing on us? You have allowed bonds of love beyond number to be painfully snapped. If you have not abandoned us, explain yourself.We strain to hear. But instead of hearing an answer we catch sight of God himself scraped and torn. Through our tears we see the tears of God.

texto: Why are the photographs of him as a little boy so incredibly hard to look at? Something is over. Now instead of those shiny moments being things we can share together in delighted memories, I, the survivor, have to bear them alone. So it is with all the memories of him. They all lead into blackness. All I can do is remember him, I cannot experience him. Nothing new can happen between us.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Nicholas Wolterstorff que provoca limites e escolhas conscientes. Ao questionar “Why are the photographs of him as a little boy so incredibly hard to look at? Something is over. …”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre familia, child-death, grief, com foco em luto e grief.
